title: The Kingï؟½s Daughter
content:
آ آ آ آ We were ten maidens in the green corn,
آ آ آ آ Small red leaves in the mill-water:
آ آ آ آ Fairer maidens never were born,
آ آ آ آ Apples of gold for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ We were ten maidens by a well-head,
آ آ آ آ Small white birds in the mill-water:
آ آ آ آ Sweeter maidens never were wed,
آ آ آ آ Rings of red for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ The first to spin, the second to sing,
آ آ آ آ Seeds of wheat in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ The third may was a goodly thing,
آ آ آ آ White bread and brown for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ The fourth to sew and the fifth to play,
آ آ آ آ Fair green weed in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ The sixth may was a goodly may,
آ آ آ آ White wine and red for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ The seventh to woo, the eighth to wed,
آ آ آ آ Fair thin reeds in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ The ninth had gold work on her head,
آ آ آ آ Honey in the comb for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ The ninth had gold work round her hair,
آ آ آ آ Fallen flowers in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ The tenth may was goodly and fair,
آ آ آ آ Golden gloves for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ We were ten maidens in a field green,
آ آ آ آ Fallen fruit in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ Fairer maidens never have been,
آ آ آ آ Golden sleeves for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ By there comes the kingï؟½s young son,
آ آ آ آ A little wind in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Out of ten maidens yeï؟½ll grant me one,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ A crown of red for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Out of ten mays yeï؟½ll give me the best,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ A little rain in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ A bed of yellow straw for all the rest,
آ آ آ آ A bed of gold for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s taï؟½en out the goodliest,
آ آ آ آ Rain that rains in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ A comb of yellow shell for all the rest,
آ آ آ آ A comb of gold for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s made her bed to the goodliest,
آ آ آ آ Wind and hail in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ A grass girdle for all the rest,
آ آ آ آ A girdle of arms for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s set his heart to the goodliest,
آ آ آ آ Snow that snows in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ Nine little kisses for all the rest,
آ آ آ آ An hundredfold for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s taï؟½en his leave at the goodliest,
آ آ آ آ Broken boats in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ Golden gifts for all the rest,
آ آ آ آ Sorrow of heart for the kingï؟½s daughter.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Yeï؟½ll make a grave for my fair body,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Running rain in the mill-water;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And yeï؟½ll streek my brother at the side of me,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ The pains of hell for the kingï؟½s daughter.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Algernon Charles Swinburne represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Algernon Charles Swinburne's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
